title: The hives, if I take them
summary: In a Slovenian village, an aunt who has kept bees for thirty years sits with her niece on the bench beside the apiary. They are deciding whether the niece will inherit the hives.

body.speaker: Aunt Marjeta
body.text: Sit. The bench is dry today. I cleaned the front of the apiary, so the bees are calmer than usual. Listen first, before you say anything.

body.speaker: Niece Eva
body.text: I have been thinking about it all week, Teta. I want to be honest. I am not sure I can do what you do. I have read the books you sent. They scared me a little.

body.speaker: Aunt Marjeta
body.text: Good. If the books had not scared you, I would worry. They make it sound like a science. It is partly a science. But mostly it is paying attention. Every morning. For thirty years I have walked out here before coffee.

body.speaker: Niece Eva
body.text: That is what worries me. I live in Ljubljana. The drive is two hours, and my work is not flexible. If I take the hives, they would be alone four or five days a week.

body.speaker: Aunt Marjeta
body.text: Then we have to be honest. You cannot keep them the way I have kept them. Twelve hives, the painted fronts, the old way — that needs someone who lives here. If you tried, you would lose half in the first bad spring, and you would blame yourself.

body.speaker: Niece Eva
body.text: So you think I should say no?

body.speaker: Aunt Marjeta
body.text: I did not say that. I said you cannot keep them this way. There is another way. Four hives, not twelve. Modern boxes, not the painted ones. Stronger bee strains that forgive a missing weekend. You would still be a beekeeper. Just a different kind from me.

body.speaker: Niece Eva
body.text: But the painted fronts — Teta, they are the whole point. Grandfather painted them. If I take four modern boxes, I am not continuing anything. I am just starting something new with your honey jars on it.

body.speaker: Aunt Marjeta
body.text: That is the question. I have thought about it for two years. The painted fronts are objects. The bees are not. If you keep four hives alive, well, with your own attention, you are continuing something. If you take twelve painted hives and they die in your second year, you have continued nothing. You have only kept the wood.

body.speaker: Niece Eva
body.text: And the painted fronts? What happens to them?

body.speaker: Aunt Marjeta
body.text: The museum in Radovljica has asked twice. I always said no, because they were still in use. If you take four modern boxes, the painted fronts go to the museum, where children will see them. That is not the worst ending.

body.speaker: Niece Eva
body.text: Can I think for a week? I want to drive home, look at my flat, look at my calendar, and ask myself whether I can really go out before work on a Saturday to check four hives. If I cannot, I would rather say no than say yes badly.

body.speaker: Aunt Marjeta
body.text: Take two weeks. Take a month. The hives are not going anywhere this summer. And Eva — if the answer is no, that is also fine. I would rather you say no clearly than take them out of duty. Bees can tell. They really can.

annotations.meaningVi: mặt trước thùng ong vẽ tay (đặc trưng Slovenia)
tha cho việc vắng mặt một cuối tuần
tiếp nối được điều gì cả
đồ vật / vật thể (không có sự sống)
bằng sự quan tâm của chính cháu
Radovljica (thị trấn Slovenia có Bảo tàng Nuôi ong)
đồng ý một cách nửa vời / không dứt khoát
Đàn ong cảm nhận được đấy.

grammarNotes.title: Second & third conditionals + would for hypothetical futures
grammarNotes.bodyVi: B1 dùng câu điều kiện loại 2 cho tình huống giả định ở tương lai: 'If I take the hives, they would be alone four or five days a week.' / 'If you tried, you would lose half of them...' Câu điều kiện loại 3 cho quá khứ không thật: 'If the books had not scared you, I would worry.' (rút gọn — tương đương 'I would have worried'). 'Would rather + V' diễn tả lựa chọn ưu tiên: 'I would rather say no than say yes badly.' / 'I would rather you say no clearly than take them out of duty.'
grammarNotes.bodyEn: B1 uses the 2nd conditional for hypothetical futures: 'If I take the hives, they would be alone four or five days a week.' / 'If you tried, you would lose half of them...' The 3rd conditional appears for unreal past: 'If the books had not scared you, I would worry.' (slightly reduced — close to 'I would have worried'). 'Would rather + V' expresses preference: 'I would rather say no than say yes badly.' / 'I would rather you say no clearly than take them out of duty.'

translationVi:
Aunt Marjeta: Ngồi đi. Băng ghế khô hôm nay. Sáng nay cô đã lau mặt trước trại ong, nên đàn ong yên hơn ngày thường. Cháu nghe trước đã, trước khi nói gì.

Niece Eva: Cả tuần cháu đã nghĩ về chuyện này, Teta. Cháu muốn thành thật. Cháu không chắc mình làm được như cô. Cháu đã đọc các cuốn sách cô gửi. Chúng làm cháu hơi sợ.

Aunt Marjeta: Tốt. Nếu sách không làm cháu sợ, cô mới lo. Sách làm nó nghe như một môn khoa học. Một phần đúng là khoa học. Nhưng phần lớn là chú tâm. Mỗi sáng. Ba mươi năm cô ra đây trước cả cà phê. Đàn ong sẽ nói cho cháu biết có gì sai, nếu cháu nhìn.

Niece Eva: Đó là điều làm cháu lo. Cháu sống ở Ljubljana. Cháu không thể ra đây trước cà phê. Lái xe hai tiếng, và công việc cháu không linh hoạt. Nếu cháu nhận, đàn ong sẽ ở một mình bốn năm ngày một tuần.

Aunt Marjeta: Vậy ta phải thành thật về điều đó. Cháu không thể giữ chúng theo cách cô đã giữ. Mười hai thùng, mặt trước vẽ tay, cách cổ truyền — cần một người sống ở đây. Nếu cháu thử, cháu sẽ mất một nửa trong mùa xuân xấu đầu tiên, và cháu sẽ tự trách.

Niece Eva: Vậy cô nghĩ cháu nên nói không?

Aunt Marjeta: Cô không nói thế. Cô nói cháu không thể giữ chúng theo cách này. Có cách khác. Bốn thùng, không phải mười hai. Thùng kiểu hiện đại, không phải loại vẽ. Dòng ong khoẻ hơn, tha cho việc vắng một cuối tuần. Cháu vẫn là người nuôi ong. Chỉ là một kiểu khác cô thôi.

Niece Eva: Nhưng các mặt vẽ — Teta, đó mới là cái cốt. Ông ngoại vẽ chúng. Bà nội ông vẽ một nửa trước đó. Nếu cháu nhận bốn thùng hiện đại, cháu không tiếp nối gì cả. Cháu chỉ bắt đầu một thứ mới với lọ mật của cô dán nhãn.

Aunt Marjeta: Câu hỏi đúng là vậy. Cô đã nghĩ hai năm. Đây là chỗ cô đi đến. Mặt vẽ là đồ vật. Đàn ong thì không. Nếu cháu giữ bốn thùng sống tốt, với sự quan tâm của cháu — kể cả trong thùng hiện đại — cháu đang tiếp nối điều gì đó. Nếu cháu nhận mười hai thùng vẽ và chúng chết vào năm thứ hai, cháu không tiếp nối gì. Cháu chỉ giữ gỗ thôi.

Niece Eva: Và các mặt vẽ? Chúng sẽ ra sao?

Aunt Marjeta: Bảo tàng ở Radovljica đã xin hai lần. Cô luôn từ chối, vì chúng còn đang dùng. Nếu cháu nhận bốn thùng hiện đại, các mặt vẽ về bảo tàng, nơi trẻ con sẽ ngắm. Không phải kết cục tệ. Chúng được làm ra để vừa là chỗ ong ở vừa được ngắm.

Niece Eva: Cháu xin một tuần suy nghĩ được không? Cháu không muốn trả lời hôm nay. Cháu muốn lái về, nhìn căn hộ, nhìn lịch của mình, và tự hỏi liệu cháu có thật sự ra trước giờ làm thứ Bảy để kiểm tra bốn thùng. Nếu không thể, cháu thà nói không còn hơn nói có một cách nửa vời.

Aunt Marjeta: Cháu lấy hai tuần. Một tháng cũng được. Đàn ong không đi đâu mùa hè này. Và Eva — nếu câu trả lời là không, cũng không sao. Cô thà cháu nói không rõ ràng hơn là nhận vì nghĩa vụ. Đàn ong cảm nhận được đấy. Thật đấy.

questions.prompt: Why did Marjeta clean the front of the apiary that morning?
options: So the bees would be calmer than usual while they talked
Because Eva was bringing a friend

questions.prompt: Why is Marjeta glad the books scared Eva?
options: Because if they had not, she would worry — beekeeping is partly science but mostly paying attention
She lives in Ljubljana, the drive is two hours and her work is not flexible — the hives would be alone four or five days a week

questions.prompt: Why can Eva not keep them the old way?
options: Twelve painted hives need someone who lives there; trying anyway would lose half in the first bad spring
Because the bees are too old
Four hives, not twelve; modern boxes, not painted ones; stronger bee strains that forgive a missing weekend

questions.prompt: What is Eva's objection to that alternative?
options: The painted fronts are the whole point — grandfather and great-grandmother painted them; four modern boxes are not continuing anything
Bees do not like new wood

questions.prompt: How does Marjeta resolve the painted-fronts question?
options: Painted fronts are objects, bees are not; four hives kept alive continue something; twelve painted hives that die continue nothing
She insists Eva keep all twelve

questions.prompt: What is the plan for the painted fronts if Eva takes four modern boxes?
options: They go to the museum in Radovljica, which has asked twice; children will see them
They will stay in the garden
Eva will keep them at her flat

questions.prompt: Why has Marjeta refused the museum until now?
options: Because the painted fronts were still in use
Because the museum is too far

questions.prompt: What does Eva ask for at the end?
options: A week to drive home, look at her flat and calendar, and ask whether she can really check four hives on a Saturday

questions.prompt: What does Marjeta say about a possible 'no'?
options: She would rather Eva say no clearly than take them out of duty — bees can tell
She would never speak to her again
She would give them to a neighbour immediately
Inheriting a craft honestly may mean changing its form to suit the inheritor's real life, so that the living part is continued — even if some treasured objects are passed on elsewhere.
Traditions must be kept exactly as they were.
Young people should refuse all inheritances.

cloze.template:
Aunt Marjeta: Sit. The bench is dry today. I cleaned the front of the {{b1}}, so the bees are calmer than usual. Listen first, before you say anything.
Niece Eva: I have been thinking about it all week, Teta. I want to be honest. I am not sure I can do what you do. I have read the books you sent. They scared me a little.
Aunt Marjeta: Good. If the books had not scared you, I would worry. They make it sound like a science. It is partly a science. But mostly it is paying {{b2}}. Every morning. For thirty years I have walked out here before coffee.
Niece Eva: That is what worries me. I live in Ljubljana. The drive is two hours, and my work is not {{b3}}. If I take the hives, they would be alone four or five days a week.
Aunt Marjeta: Then we have to be honest. You cannot keep them the way I have kept them. Twelve hives, the painted fronts, the old way — that needs someone who lives here. If you tried, you would {{b4}} half in the first bad spring, and you would blame yourself.
Niece Eva: So you think I should say no?
Aunt Marjeta: I did not say that. I said you cannot keep them this way. There is another way. Four hives, not twelve. Modern boxes, not the painted ones. Stronger bee {{b5}} that forgive a missing weekend. You would still be a beekeeper. Just a different kind from me.
Niece Eva: But the painted fronts — Teta, they are the whole point. Grandfather painted them. If I take four modern boxes, I am not continuing anything. I am just starting something new with your honey {{b6}} on it.
Aunt Marjeta: That is the question. I have thought about it for two years. The painted fronts are {{b7}}. The bees are not. If you keep four hives alive, well, with your own attention, you are continuing something. If you take twelve painted hives and they die in your second year, you have continued nothing. You have only kept the wood.
Niece Eva: And the painted fronts? What happens to them?
Aunt Marjeta: The {{b8}} in Radovljica has asked twice. I always said no, because they were still in use. If you take four modern boxes, the painted fronts go to the museum, where children will see them. That is not the worst ending.
Niece Eva: Can I think for a week? I want to drive home, look at my flat, look at my {{b9}}, and ask myself whether I can really go out before work on a Saturday to check four hives. If I cannot, I would rather say no than say {{b10}} badly.
Aunt Marjeta: Take two weeks. Take a month. The hives are not going anywhere this summer. And Eva — if the answer is no, that is also fine. I would rather you say no clearly than take them out of {{b11}}. Bees can tell. They really can.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Marjeta says objects can move on while the living part is continued. Think of something you might one day inherit — a recipe, a tool, a routine. What would have to change in the form for the living part to survive in your life?
